questions.question: Define registers and explain their role in CPU operation.
keyPoints: Registers are small, high-speed memory locations within the CPU
They store data, instructions, and addresses during processing
Provide fastest access time in memory hierarchy
Used by ALU for arithmetic and logic operations
Examples: Accumulator, Program Counter, Instruction Register

questions.question: Distinguish between SRAM and DRAM.
keyPoints: SRAM: Static RAM uses flip-flop circuits, no refresh needed, faster but expensive, used in cache
DRAM: Dynamic RAM uses capacitors, needs periodic refresh, slower but cheaper, used in main memory
SRAM: 6 transistors per bit, DRAM: 1 capacitor + 1 transistor per bit
DRAM offers higher density, SRAM offers better speed

questions.question: Explain the types of ROM with their characteristics.
keyPoints: Mask ROM: Factory-programmed, cannot be modified
PROM: Programmably written once after manufacturing
EPROM: Erasable using UV light, can be reprogrammed
EEPROM: Electrically erasable and programmable, in-circuit update possible
Flash Memory: Block-based erasure, used in USB drives and SSDs

questions.question: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory? Give examples.
keyPoints: Volatile: Loses data when power is removed - examples: RAM, Cache, Registers
Non-volatile: Retains data without power - examples: ROM, Flash, Hard Disk
Volatile used for temporary storage during operation
Non-volatile used for permanent storage of data and programs
Modern systems use both types in memory hierarchy

questions.question: Explain the concept of memory hierarchy in computer systems.
keyPoints: Memory hierarchy organizes storage by speed and cost
Top: CPU Registers (fastest, most expensive)
Then: Cache Memory (L1, L2, L3)
Then: Main Memory (RAM)
Bottom: Secondary Storage (HDD, SSD)
Goal: Balance speed, cost, and capacity

questions.question: What is cache memory? Explain its purpose and levels.
keyPoints: Cache is small, fast memory between CPU and RAM
Stores frequently accessed data and instructions
Reduces CPU wait time for memory operations
L1 Cache: Closest to CPU, smallest, fastest
L2 Cache: Larger but slower than L1
L3 Cache: Shared among cores, largest, slowest of cache levels

questions.question: Explain the difference between RAM and ROM. Discuss their types, characteristics, and applications in computer systems.
keyPoints: RAM: Volatile, read-write memory for temporary storage of data and programs
ROM: Non-volatile, primarily read-only for permanent storage
RAM Types: SRAM (fast, expensive, cache), DRAM (slower, cheaper, main memory)
ROM Types: PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash with varying programmability
RAM applications: Running programs, active data storage
ROM applications: BIOS, firmware, embedded systems
Both essential in computer architecture for different purposes

questions.question: Describe the internal organization of computer memory. Explain address bus, data bus, and their relationship with memory capacity and word size.
keyPoints: Memory organized as array of addressable locations
Each location stores one word of data
Address bus carries addresses from CPU to memory
Data bus carries actual data between CPU and memory
Address bus width determines maximum addressable memory (2^n locations)
Data bus width determines word size (bits transferred per operation
Example: 16-bit address bus can address 64KB, 32-bit can address 4GB
Memory capacity = 2^(address bits) × word size in bytes

questions.question: Explain how cache memory improves system performance. Discuss cache mapping techniques.
keyPoints: Cache bridges speed gap between CPU and main memory
Stores copy of frequently accessed data
Reduces memory access latency
Three mapping techniques: Direct Mapping, Fully Associative, Set Associative
Direct Mapping: Each block maps to one cache line - simple but potential conflicts
Fully Associative: Any block can go anywhere - most flexible but expensive
Set Associative: Compromise - blocks mapped to sets, lines within sets are associative
Cache hit: data found in cache, Cache miss: need to fetch from RAM

questions.question: Discuss the characteristics and applications of PROM, EPROM, and EEPROM.
keyPoints: PROM: Programmable ROM, programmed once using PROM programmer, cannot be erased or reprogrammed
EPROM: Erasable PROM, erased using ultraviolet light through quartz window, can be reprogrammed multiple times
EEPROM: Electrically erasable, can be erased and reprogrammed in-circuit without removing chip
Applications: EPROM used in development/prototyping, EEPROM for BIOS updates and small data storage
Flash Memory: Evolved from EEPROM, block-based erasure, used in USB drives, SSDs, memory cards
Advantages of EEPROM over EPROM: Selective byte-level erasure, in-circuit programming, no UV exposure needed
